description: I first saw a medium-sized yellow-bellied flycatcher fly in from the south and land in the top of a mature upright juniper shrub.  It was immediately recognizable as a kingbird.  Since it was my first kingbird of any kind this year, I obviously took note of it.  The first thing that struck me as odd was the lack of white on the outer tail when it flared its tail to land.  The tail appeared unformly brown with narrow pale feather tips.  I next noticed a head pattern that did not fit with the expected Cassin's Kingbird, given the appearance of the tail.  The head was light gray with a slightly darker auricular area (aka "mask").  This pattern maybe contrasted a bit more than would be expected in Western but did not have the heavier mask typical of Cassin's, nor the stark white malar/throat contrast seen in Cassin's.  Looking at the throat, I noticed it was light gray with a greenish yellow (not gray) upper chest.  The rest of the underparts below the greenish-yellow upper chest was bright yellow.  The bill also appeared a bit thicker and perhaps a bit longer than I would expect in Western, but not as thin and long as Tropical Kingbird.  I was beginning to suspect Couch's at this point.  The bird flew to the top of another juniper and caught something large, which it ate rather quickly with its head down and I could never make out.  It might have been a darner dragonfly.  I moved to its location on the north side of the ditch to try for closer observations/photographs.  The bird flew to the south side of the ditch, flew behind a big juniper, and I never saw it again.  Finishing out the description of this yellow kingbird, its back was pale greenish-gray but not as bright green as the field guides and my experience indicate is typical of Tropical/Couch's.  In fact I have seen Western Kingbirds with greener backs than this bird had.  That is the only field mark that is not in keeping with a slam-dunk ID of Couch's.  The vocalizations I heard were infrequent and rather quiet, not at all aggitated or hyper, and were mostly single notes that sounded like "kit" or "kip".  They were not unlike notes I have heard from Western but also would seem to be good for Couch's.
photographer: Photographer: David Leatherman, David Leatherman, David Leatherman, David Leatherman
